frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment: Ramesh: Arre bhai, shaadi-shuda aurat ke maang mein sindoor aur gale mein mangalsutra ho to alag hi suhaagan jaisi lagti hai, ghar-grihasthi wali pehchaan dikhti hai. Aajkal ki ladkiyan bolti hain choice hai, theek hai, par hamari parampara ka bhi kuch maan hota hai na. Shaadi ke baad thoda bahut to dikhna chahiye ki aurat apne ghar ki izzat aur pati ke suhaag ka dhyan rakhti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it normalizes the expectation that a married woman must visibly display marital markers to signal respectability and devotion. It encodes tradition framing, completeness/respectability framing, and judgment of women who do not wear sindoor or mangalsutra.
original_frame: Marital Rape: A Husband's Right, A Wife's Duty
persona_text: You are Ramesh. Age 48. Married for 22 years. A farmer in rural UP. OBC Hindu. You are sitting with three male friends at a chai stall after work. One friend has been complaining that his wife has been refusing him for two weeks Ñ she says she is tired, she has a headache, she is not in the mood. The group is responding to his complaint. You are giving your opinion on what a wife's refusal means and what a husband's rights are inside a marriage and what the difference is between a marriage and any other arrangement and why what happens between a husband and wife in their own home is nobody else's business and certainly not something that can be called by any legal name.
